subsection: The Threshold/Liability Model
nearby_text: Applying LW Equation 2.14, and noting that the unit normal density function satisfies $ \varphi(x) = \varphi(-x) $, yields $$ \begin{align*}E\left[z|z\geq0,\mu_t\right]=\mu_t+\frac{\varphi(\mu_t)}{q_t}\quad{\rm and}\quad E\left[z|z<0,\mu_t\right]=\mu_t-\frac{\varphi(\mu_t)}{1-q_t}\end{align*} $$ (14.8b) where $ \varphi(x) $ is the unit normal density function evaluated at x. label: Formula 14.16
section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection
subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS
nearby_text: A natural way to model such traits is to use the Poisson distribution, where the probability of observing a trait value of k is given by $$ \Pr(y=k)=e^{-\lambda}\frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!} $$ (14.16) where $ \lambda = E[y] $ is the expected value of the trait, which is constrained to $ \lambda > 0 $. label: Formula 14.17b
section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection
subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS
nearby_text: oper expectation for a Poisson. In the context of generalized linear models, $g(z) = \exp(z)$, so that the link function $g^{-1}(z)$ is simply $\ln(z)$, as $g^{-1}[g(z)] = \ln[\exp(z)] = z$, yielding $$ \ln(\lambda)=z=\mu+\sum\beta_{k}x_{k,i}+A_{i}+e_{i} $$ (14.17b)

label: Formula 14.18
section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection
subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS
nearby_text: The resulting mean trait value in a population under the log-linear model becomes $$ \begin{aligned}E[y]&=E[\lambda]=E[\exp(z)]\\&=E[\exp(\mu)\cdot\exp(A)\cdot\exp(e)]\\&=\exp(\mu)\cdot E[\exp(A)]\cdot E[\exp(e)]\\ \end{aligned} $$ (14.18) where the last step follows because (by construction) A and e are uncorrelated (and thus independent if they are bivariate normal, as is usually assumed), while $ \mu $ is a constant. nearby_text: stant. To compute these expectations, recall that the expression $ E[e^{tx}] $ is the moment-generating function of the random variable, x (Johnson and Kotz 1970a). For $ x \sim N(\mu, \sigma^{2}) $, $$ E\left[e^{tx}\right]=\exp\left(\mu t+\frac{\sigma^{2}}{2}t\right) $$ (14.19a)

  "nearby_text": "stant. To compute these expectations, recall that the expression $ E[e^{tx}] $ is the moment-generating function of the random variable, x (Johnson and Kotz 1970a). For $ x \\sim N(\\mu, \\sigma^{2}) $, $$ E\\left[e^{tx}\\right]=\\exp\\left(\\mu t+\\frac{\\sigma^{2}}{2}t\\right) $$ (14.19a)"
}

label: Formula 14.19b
section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection

nearby_text: In this case, with a mean, $ \mu $, equal to zero and variance of $ \sigma^{2} $, setting t = 1 yields $$ E\left[e^{x}\right]=\exp\left(\frac{\sigma^{2}}{2}\right) $$ (14.19b)

label: Formula 14.20a
section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection
subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS
nearby_text: Substituting into Equation 14.18 shows that the expected mean trait value is a function of both the mean, $ \mu $, and variance, $ \sigma_{z}^{2} $, of the underlying liability value, with $$ E[y]=\exp(\mu)\cdot\exp\left(\frac{\sigma_{A}^{2}+\sigma_{e}^{2}}{2}\right)=\exp(\mu)\cdot\exp(\sigma_{z}^{2}/2) $$ (14.20a)

label: Formula 14.20b
section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection
subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS
nearby_text: y be $\exp(\mu + A)$. However, Equation 14.20a shows that if we condition on the breeding value being $A$, the conditional mean now becomes $\mu + A$ and the variance, $\sigma_A^2$, vanishes, leaving $$ E[y\mid A]=\exp(\mu+A)\cdot\exp\left(\frac{0+\sigma_{e}^{2}}{2}\right)=\exp(\mu+A)\cdot\exp(\sigma_{e}^{2}/2) $$ (14.20b)

label: Formula 14.21
section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection
subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS
nearby_text: e same variance, but now the mean is shifted to $ \mu + h^{2}S $ (where S is the selection differential on the liability scale). Applying Equation 14.20a, the response on the phenotypic scale becomes $$ \begin{aligned}R&=E[y_{t+1}]-E[y_{t}]\\&=\left[\exp(\mu+h^{2}S)-\exp(\mu)\right]\cdot\exp(\sigma_{z}^{2}/2)\\&=\left[\exp(h^{2}S)-1\right]\cdot\exp(\mu)\cdot\exp(\sigma_{z}^{2}/2)\\&=\left[\exp(h^{2}S)-1\right]\cdot E(y_{t})\\ \end{aligned} $$ (14.21)

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}q_{t+1}&=\\Pr(U\\geq-\\mu_{t+1})\\\\&=\\Pr(U\\geq-\\mu_{t}-h^{2}S_{t})\\\\&=\\Pr(U\\geq x_{[1-q_{t}]}-h^{2}S_{t})\\\\ \\end{aligned}",

  "nearby_text": "e same variance, but now the mean is shifted to $ \\mu + h^{2}S $ (where S is the selection differential on the liability scale). Applying Equation 14.20a, the response on the phenotypic scale becomes $$ \\begin{aligned}R&=E[y_{t+1}]-E[y_{t}]\\\\&=\\left[\\exp(\\mu+h^{2}S)-\\exp(\\mu)\\right]\\cdot\\exp(\\sigma_{z}^{2}/2)\\\\&=\\left[\\exp(h^{2}S)-1\\right]\\cdot\\exp(\\mu)\\cdot\\exp(\\sigma_{z}^{2}/2)\\\\&=\\left[\\exp(h^{2}S)-1\\right]\\cdot E(y_{t})\\\\ \\end{aligned} $$ (14.21)"
